dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unknown problem/person down at Bus Station on Ford Ave in [CITY].
Hi, my name is Chris, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what made you call 911?
We found a man on the ground, not responding. He slid off the bench.
Does he respond to loud noises or light tapping on the shoulder?
No, he does not. We tried shaking his shoulders.
Partner, check if his eyes open to pain.
No eye movement witnessed during nail-bed pressure tests. Eyes remain closed.
No verbal response and groans are not intelligible.
No spontaneous movement has been observed.
Airway appears compromised with sonorous respirations. Partner, assist ventilation with BVM and apply O2.
Respiratory rate is 6 per minute, slowed and shallow with no signs of respiratory labor.
Room air SpO2 is 41%, which is very low.
Breathing inadequate, need for assisted ventilation. Partner, use BVM with an E/C grip.
Blood pressure is unreadable with current method. Partner, check again after confirming positioning.
Airway compromised. Use head-tilt-chin-lift. Partner, prepare the nasal pharyngeal airway.
NPA inserted into the right nostril successfully. BVM with O2 connected to 15LPM for ventilation.
Carotid pulse is strong and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and its color is normal.
Pulse rate is 100 beats per minute and consistent.
Blood pressure is 0/0, technically non-palpable or unobtainable.
Blood glucose meter returned no usable reading at this time; result is 0.
Partner, check breath sounds on both sides. Bystander, have you observed any chest trauma?
He just slid and landed on the ground; we don't observe any chest trauma.
Breathing sounds are decreased but audible and equal. No wheezing or crackles present.
4-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm at this time. Cardiac monitoring in progress.
Breathing remains poor with oxygen saturation at 41%. Partner, secure IV access and monitor the patient.
IV placed at the external jugular vein successfully. A saline lock has been affixed for possible med administration.
Administering 0.25mg of Naloxone via IV after a two-provider medication cross-check is done. Monitor for a response.
Blood glucose is now 162 mmol/L, which is within the normal range.
SpO2 increased to 94%. Improvement in respiratory status is observed.
GCS is improving to E1, V1, M1. No movement or vocalizations to command, but eyelids open to pressure.
Mr. David, can you tell me what you were doing before you fell and if you've used any opioids?
I'm David. I don't remember using anything lately. No pain pills or heroin.
What were you doing before this happened? Did you feel any weakness or dizziness?
I was sitting at a bench, then fell. I felt some nausea but not anything else.
Are you allergic to anything or have any medical history we should know about?
I'm not allergic to any medications. I take Melatonin for sleep and no other pills recently.
Skin is dry, warm, and intact with no signs of trauma or rash.
Pupils are 2 mm, equal, and reactive to light, but very pinpoint in appearance.
Respiratory rate is now 12 per minute with improved depth and smooth character.
Blood pressure now is 201/88, noted after initial exposure to O2 and Naloxone.
Glucose levels remain at 162, still considered normal.
Current EKG now shows sinus tachycardia-heart rate is elevated but rhythm is stable.
Radial pulse is now strong and regular after the administration of Naloxone.
On a scale of 0-10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate your present discomfort?
I rate my pain at 0. I don't feel any discomfort currently.
Naloxone has been effective. Breathing and mental state are improving.
SpO2 is now 99%, pulse is at 102. Continue as needed.
Respiratory rate appears to have decreased to 10 per minute. Could reduce O2 flow slightly.
Blood pressure is now 174/121 with a pulse elevated to 107.
EKG still showing sinus tachycardia. No dangerous arrhythmias detected.
Patient's arms and legs are secured using cravats for movement safety.
We need to transport him to the hospital. Any preference in facilities?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine with me.
Opioid Overdose Protocol (7-1) steps taken and documented. Continue monitoring during transport.
No major updates from the last assessment. ETCO2 levels are within the normal range.
SpO2 is 94% while on 4 liters of O2. Monitoring as required.
Just to check, no new pain, nausea, or change in mental status reported.
No further medications are needed at this point. IV remains functional.
All limbs are restrained with a 4-point approach for transport security.
Getting everything ready for transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Respiratory rate is now 10 per minute with better air movement since Naloxone.
SpO2 is at 100% following interventions-now reaching acceptable range.
Observation of accessory muscles in use, suggest increased breathing effort.
Radial pulse is still strong and regular.
Final blood pressure reading before transport is 174/121.
Breath sounds now equal and clear, no adventitious sounds noted.
No masses or lymphadenopathy seen in the head and neck area.
Monitor remains in sinus tach with no new cardiac rhythm deviations.
Continue O2 via nasal cannula at 4LPM. Partner, ready the stretcher.
All limbs are secured with restraints confirmed.
Applied four cravats to secure all four extremities for safety during transport.
Patient secure with no abnormal head or neck movement.
Raised patient safely onto stretcher with team support.
Correct handling techniques used to transfer into the ambulance.
Vital signs trend downward with continued improvement.
Double-checked restraints for proper use during transit.
Positioned the stretcher in a safe location for transport.
Confirmed all ambulance doors are secured for travel.
Ready to depart upon confirmation from all team members.
Verified the operational status of O2 and cardiac monitor for travel.
Departing to hospital. All on board prepared for the next steps.
GPS navigation set for [COMPANY_NAME] with fastest route selected.
No new data needed or patient updates for hospital records.
No new medications or procedures required for now.
Continuous EKG monitoring initiated upon securing the patient.
SpO2 remains steady at 100%.
Respiratory rate is now 10 per minute, post-Naloxone intervention.
Blood pressure is elevated slightly, no signs of distress.
Hospital has been notified of ECG findings and medications administered so far.
Mental status consistent with AAOx1 following Naloxone administration.
Final vital signs before arrival-B/P 174/121, RR 10, HR 102, SpO2 100%.
Double-check IV site and cap integrity for transit.
Medications are clearly labeled and ready for handoff.
No adverse events observed from procedures or medication administration.
Estimated time of arrival at RCH is about 20 minutes.
Ensure airway remains unobstructed during transit.
Monitor EKG for comparison to baseline readings regularly.
IV secured and operational during movement, no dislodgement.
Second assessment confirms stable patient condition during transit.
Respiratory rate is 8 per minute during final reassessment pre-arrival.
